([US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˈsɑːmi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SAH-mee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): also [/ˈsæmi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SAM-ee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) also rendered in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") as **Sami** and **Saami**, are a group of [Uralic languages](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") spoken by the indigenous [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people") people in [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe") (in parts of northern [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), and extreme northwestern [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia")). There are, depending on the nature and terms of division, ten or more Sami languages. Several spellings have been used for the Sámi languages, including *Sámi*, *Sami*, *Saami*, *Saame*, *Sámic*, *Samic* and *Saamic*, as well as the [exonyms](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym") **Lappish** and *Lappic*. The last two, along with the term *Lapp*, are now often considered [pejorative](/wiki/Pejorative "Pejorative").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

**This article contains [special characters](/wiki/Help:Special_characters "Help:Special characters").** Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Special_characters "Help:Special characters"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)").

The **Sámi languages** ([US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˈsɑːmi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SAH-mee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): also [/ˈsæmi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SAM-ee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) also rendered in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") as **Sami** and **Saami**, are a group of [Uralic languages](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") spoken by the indigenous [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people") people in [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe") (in parts of northern [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), and extreme northwestern [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia")). There are, depending on the nature and terms of division, ten or more Sami languages. Several spellings have been used for the Sámi languages, including *Sámi*, *Sami*, *Saami*, *Saame*, *Sámic*, *Samic* and *Saamic*, as well as the [exonyms](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym") **Lappish** and *Lappic*. The last two, along with the term *Lapp*, are now often considered [pejorative](/wiki/Pejorative "Pejorative").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Classification")]

The Sámi languages form a branch of the [Uralic language family](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages"). According to the traditional view, Sámi is within the Uralic family most closely related to the [Finnic languages](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages") (Sammallahti 1998). However, this view has recently been doubted by some scholars who argue that the traditional view of a common [Finno-Sami](/wiki/Finno-Samic_languages "Finno-Samic languages") protolanguage is not as strongly supported as had been earlier assumed,[[6]](#cite_note-6) and that the similarities may stem from an [areal](/wiki/Areal_feature "Areal feature") influence on Sámi from Finnic.

In terms of internal relationships, the Sámi languages are traditionally divided into the two groups of western and eastern. The groups may be further divided into various subgroups and ultimately individual languages. (Sammallahti 1998: 6-38.) Recently it has been proposed on the basis of (1) different sound substitutions seen between the Sámi languages in the Proto-Scandinavian loanwords and (2) historical phonology that the first unit to branch off from Late Proto-Sámi was Southern Proto-Sámi, from which descend South Sámi, Ume Sámi, and Gävle Sámi (extinct during the 19th century).[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Parts of the Sámi language area form a [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum") in which the neighbouring languages may be [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") to a fair degree, but two more widely separated groups will not understand each other's speech. There are, however, some sharp language boundaries, in particular between [Northern Sami](/wiki/Northern_Sami "Northern Sami"), [Inari Sami](/wiki/Inari_S%C3%A1mi_language "Inari Sámi language") and [Skolt Sami](/wiki/Skolt_Sami_language "Skolt Sami language"), the speakers of which are not able to understand each other without learning or long practice. The evolution of sharp language boundaries seems to suggest a relative isolation of the language speakers from each other and not very intensive contacts between the respective speakers in the past. There is some significance in this, as the geographical barriers between the respective speakers are no different from those in other parts of the Sámi area.

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

The Sami languages are spoken in [Sápmi](/wiki/S%C3%A1pmi "Sápmi") in [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe"), in a region stretching over the four countries [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") and [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), reaching from the southern part of central [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") in the southwest to the tip of the [Kola Peninsula](/wiki/Kola_Peninsula "Kola Peninsula") in the east. The borders between the languages do not align with the ones separating the region's modern states.

During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"), now-extinct Sami languages were also spoken in the central and southern parts of [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") and [Karelia](/wiki/Karelia "Karelia") and in a wider area on the [Scandinavian Peninsula](/wiki/Scandinavian_Peninsula "Scandinavian Peninsula"). Historical documents as well as [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") and [Karelian](/wiki/Karelian_language "Karelian language") [oral tradition](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition") contain many mentions of the earlier Sami inhabitation in these areas (Itkonen, 1947). Also, [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") as well as place-names of Sami origin in the southern dialects of Finnish and Karelian dialects testify of earlier Sami presence in the area (Koponen, 1996; Saarikivi, 2004; Aikio, 2007). These Sami languages, however, became extinct later, under the wave of the Finno-Karelian agricultural expansion.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

The [Proto-Sámi language](/wiki/Proto-S%C3%A1mi_language "Proto-Sámi language") is believed to have formed in the vicinity of the [Gulf of Finland](/wiki/Gulf_of_Finland "Gulf of Finland") between 1000 BC to 700 AD, deriving from a common Proto-Sami-Finnic language (M. Korhonen 1981).[[19]](#cite_note-M._Korhonen-19) However, reconstruction of any basic proto-languages in the Uralic family have reached a level close to or identical to [Proto-Uralic](/wiki/Proto-Uralic "Proto-Uralic") (Salminen 1999).[[20]](#cite_note-T._Salminen-20) According to the comparative linguist Ante Aikio, the Proto-Samic language developed in South Finland or in Karelia around 2000–2500 years ago, spreading then to northern Fennoscandia.[[21]](#cite_note-Aikio_2004-21) The language is believed to have expanded west and north into [Fennoscandia](/wiki/Fennoscandia "Fennoscandia") during the [Nordic Iron Age](/wiki/Nordic_Iron_Age "Nordic Iron Age"), reaching central [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") during the [Proto-Scandinavian](/wiki/Proto-Scandinavian "Proto-Scandinavian") period ca. 500 AD (Bergsland 1996).[[22]](#cite_note-Bergsland,_Knut-22) The language assimilated several strata of unknown [Paleo-European languages](/wiki/Paleo-European_languages "Paleo-European languages") from the early hunter-gatherers, first during the Proto-Sami phase and second in the subsequent expansion of the language in the west and the north of Fennoscandia that is part of modern [Sápmi](/wiki/S%C3%A1pmi "Sápmi") today. (Aikio 2004, Aikio 2006).[[21]](#cite_note-Aikio_2004-21)[[23]](#cite_note-Aikio_2006-23)

## Written languages and sociolinguistic situation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Written languages and sociolinguistic situation")]

At present there are nine living Sami languages. Eight of the languages have independent literary languages; the other one has no written standard, and of it, there are only a few, mainly elderly, speakers left. The [ISO 639-2](/wiki/ISO_639-2 "ISO 639-2") code for all Sami languages without their own code is "smi". The eight written languages are:

* [Northern Sami](/wiki/Northern_Sami_language "Northern Sami language") (Norway, Sweden, Finland): With an estimated 15,000 speakers, this accounts for probably more than 75% of all Sami speakers in 2002.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [ISO 639-1](/wiki/ISO_639-1 "ISO 639-1")/[ISO 639-2](/wiki/ISO_639-2 "ISO 639-2"): se/sme
* [Lule Sami](/wiki/Lule_Sami_language "Lule Sami language") (Norway, Sweden): The second largest group with an estimated 1,500 speakers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [ISO 639-2](/wiki/ISO_639-2 "ISO 639-2"): smj
* [Ume Sami](/wiki/Ume_Sami_language "Ume Sami language") (Norway, Sweden): likely has under 20 speakers left. [ISO 639-2](/wiki/ISO_639-2 "ISO 639-2"): sju
* [Pite Sami](/wiki/Pite_Sami_language "Pite Sami language") has about 30–50 speakers,[[24]](#cite_note-24) [ISO 639-2](/wiki/ISO_639-2 "ISO 639-2"): sje
* [Southern Sami](/wiki/Southern_Sami_language "Southern Sami language") (Norway, Sweden): 500 speakers (estimated).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [ISO 639-2](/wiki/ISO_639-2 "ISO 639-2"): sma
* [Inari Sami](/wiki/Inari_S%C3%A1mi_language "Inari Sámi language") (*Enare Sami*) ([Inari](/wiki/Inari,_Finland "Inari, Finland"), Finland): 500 speakers (estimated).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [SIL code](/wiki/SIL_code "SIL code"): LPI, [ISO 639-2](/wiki/ISO_639-2 "ISO 639-2"): smn
* [Skolt Sami](/wiki/Skolt_Sami_language "Skolt Sami language") (Näätämö and the Nellim-Keväjärvi districts, [Inari](/wiki/Inari,_Finland "Inari, Finland") municipality, Finland, also spoken in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), previously in Norway): 400 speakers (estimated).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [SIL code](/wiki/SIL_code "SIL code"): LPK, [ISO 639-2](/wiki/ISO_639-2 "ISO 639-2"): sms
* [Kildin Sami](/wiki/Kildin_Sami_language "Kildin Sami language") ([Kola Peninsula](/wiki/Kola_Peninsula "Kola Peninsula"), Russia): 608 speakers in [Murmansk Oblast](/wiki/Murmansk_Oblast "Murmansk Oblast"), 179 in other Russian regions, although 1991 persons stated their Saami ethnicity (1769 of them live in [Murmansk Oblast](/wiki/Murmansk_Oblast "Murmansk Oblast"))[[25]](#cite_note-25) [SIL code](/wiki/SIL_code "SIL code"): LPD, [ISO 639-3](/wiki/ISO_639-3 "ISO 639-3"): sjd

The other Sami languages are critically endangered ([moribund](/wiki/Moribund_language "Moribund language"), have very few speakers left) or extinct. Ten speakers of [Ter Sami](/wiki/Ter_Sami_language "Ter Sami language") were known to be alive in 2004.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The last speaker of [Akkala Sami](/wiki/Akkala_Sami_language "Akkala Sami language") is known to have died in December 2003,[[27]](#cite_note-27) and the eleventh attested variety, [Kemi Sami](/wiki/Kemi_Sami_language "Kemi Sami language"), became extinct in the 19th century. An additional Sami language, [Kainuu Sami](/wiki/Kainuu_Sami_language "Kainuu Sami language"), became extinct in the 18th century, and probably belonged to the Eastern group like Kemi Sami, although the evidence for the language is limited.

## Orthographies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Orthographies")]

Main article: [Sámi orthography](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_orthography "Sámi orthography")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Sami_alphabet_1933.jpg/250px-Sami_alphabet_1933.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sami_alphabet_1933.jpg)

Sami Primer, USSR 1933

Most Sámi languages use [Latin alphabets](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"), with these respective additional letters.

:   |  |  |
    | --- | --- |
    | Northern Sámi: | Áá Čč Đđ Ŋŋ Šš Ŧŧ Žž |
    | Inari Sámi: | Áá Ââ Ää Čč Đđ Ŋŋ Šš Žž |
    | Skolt Sámi: | Ââ Čč Ʒʒ Ǯǯ [Đđ](/wiki/D_with_stroke "D with stroke") Ǧǧ Ǥǥ [Ǩǩ](/wiki/%C7%A8 "Ǩ") Ŋŋ Õõ Šš Žž Åå Ää ʹ ʼ |
    | Lule Sámi (Sweden): | Áá Åå Ŋŋ Ää |
    | Lule Sámi (Norway): | Áá Åå Ŋŋ Ææ |
    | Southern Sámi (Sweden): | Ïï Ää Öö Åå |
    | Southern Sámi (Norway): | Ïï Ææ Øø Åå |
    | Ume Sámi: | Áá Đđ Ïï Ŋŋ Ŧŧ Üü Åå Ää Öö |
    | Pite Sámi: | Áá Đđ Ŋŋ Ŧŧ Åå Ää |

The use of Ææ and Øø in Norway vs. Ää and Öö in Sweden merely reflects the orthographic standards used in the [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_alphabet "Norwegian alphabet") and [Swedish alphabets](/wiki/Swedish_alphabet "Swedish alphabet"), respectively, not differences in pronunciations.

The letter Đ in Sámi languages is a capital [D with a bar](/wiki/D_with_stroke "D with stroke") across it ([Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") [code point](/wiki/Code_point "Code point"): U+0110), which is also used in [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian"), [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"), etc., not the near-identical capital [eth](/wiki/Eth "Eth") (Ð; U+00D0) used in [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language"), [Faroese](/wiki/Faroese_language "Faroese language") or [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Latin_letter_Eng_%28first_variant_of_capital_form%29.svg/250px-Latin_letter_Eng_%28first_variant_of_capital_form%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Latin_letter_Eng_(first_variant_of_capital_form).svg)

Sámi languages tend to prefer the N-form eng for the uppercase letter.

The capital letter Ŋ ([eng](/wiki/Eng_(letter) "Eng (letter)")) is commonly presented in Sámi languages using the "N-form" variant based the usual Latin uppercase N with a hook added.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Unicode assigns code point U+014A to the uppercase eng, but does not prescribe the form of the glyph.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The Skolt Sámi standard uses ʹ (U+02B9) as a soft sign,[[30]](#cite_note-30) but other apostrophes, such as ' (U+0027), ˊ (U+02CA) or ´ (U+00B4), are also sometimes used in published texts.

The [Kildin Sámi orthography](/wiki/Kildin_Sami_orthography "Kildin Sami orthography") uses the Russian [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script") with these additional letters: А̄а̄ Ӓӓ Е̄е̄ Ё̄ё̄ Һһ/ʼ Ӣӣ Јј/Ҋҋ Ӆӆ Ӎӎ Ӊӊ Ӈӈ О̄о̄ Ҏҏ Ӯӯ Ҍҍ Э̄э̄ Ӭӭ Ю̄ю̄ Я̄я̄

## Official status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Official status")]

### Norway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Norway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Norwegian_Labour_Party_shirt_in_three_Saami_languages.jpeg/250px-Norwegian_Labour_Party_shirt_in_three_Saami_languages.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Norwegian_Labour_Party_shirt_in_three_Saami_languages.jpeg)

A t-shirt for the [Norwegian Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(Norway) "Labour Party (Norway)"). From top to bottom: Northern Saami, Lule Saami, and Southern Saami.

Adopted in April 1988, Article 108 of the [Norwegian Constitution](/wiki/Norwegian_Constitution "Norwegian Constitution") states: "It is the responsibility of the authorities of the State to create conditions enabling the Sami people to preserve and develop its language, culture and way of life". The Sami Language Act went into effect in the 1990s. Sámi is an official language alongside Norwegian in the "administrative area for Sámi language", that includes eight municipalities in the northern half of Norway, namely [Kautokeino Municipality](/wiki/Kautokeino_Municipality "Kautokeino Municipality"), [Karasjok Municipality](/wiki/Karasjok_Municipality "Karasjok Municipality"), [Kåfjord Municipality](/wiki/K%C3%A5fjord_Municipality "Kåfjord Municipality"), [Nesseby Municipality](/wiki/Nesseby_Municipality "Nesseby Municipality"), [Porsanger Municipality](/wiki/Porsanger_Municipality "Porsanger Municipality"), [Tana Municipality](/wiki/Tana_Municipality "Tana Municipality"), [Tysfjord Municipality](/wiki/Tysfjord_Municipality "Tysfjord Municipality"), [Lavangen Municipality](/wiki/Lavangen_Municipality "Lavangen Municipality"), and [Snåsa Municipality](/wiki/Sn%C3%A5sa_Municipality "Snåsa Municipality").[[34]](#cite_note-34) In 2005 Sámi, [Kven](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language"), [Romanes](/wiki/Romanes "Romanes") and [Romani](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language") were recognised as "regional or minority languages" in Norway within the framework of the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Sweden")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Jokkmokk_table.jpg/250px-Jokkmokk_table.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jokkmokk_table.jpg)

A trilingual road sign for [Jokkmokk](/wiki/Jokkmokk "Jokkmokk"). From top to bottom: Swedish, Lule Saami, Northern Saami

On 1 April 2000, Sami became one of five recognized [minority languages](/wiki/Minority_languages_of_Sweden "Minority languages of Sweden") in [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) It can be used in dealing with public authorities in [Arjeplog Municipality](/wiki/Arjeplog_Municipality "Arjeplog Municipality"), [Gällivare Municipality](/wiki/G%C3%A4llivare_Municipality "Gällivare Municipality"), [Jokkmokk Municipality](/wiki/Jokkmokk_Municipality "Jokkmokk Municipality"), and [Kiruna Municipality](/wiki/Kiruna_Municipality "Kiruna Municipality"). In 2011, this list was enlarged considerably. In Sweden the University of Umeå teaches North, Ume and South Sami, and Uppsala University has courses in North, Lule and South Sami.

### Finland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Finland")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/SAKK_galba.jpg/250px-SAKK_galba.jpg)](/wiki/File:SAKK_galba.jpg)

A quadrilingual street sign in [Inari](/wiki/Inari,_Finland "Inari, Finland") in (from top to bottom) Finnish, Northern Saami, Inari Saami, and Skolt Saami, saying [Sámi Regional Education Center](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_Regional_Education_Center&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sámi Regional Education Center (page does not exist)") [[fi](https://fi.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saamelaisalueen_koulutuskeskus "fi:Saamelaisalueen koulutuskeskus")]. Inari is the only municipality in Finland with 4 official languages.

In [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), the Sami language act of 1991 granted the Northern, Inari, and Skolt Sami the right to use their languages for all government services. The *Sami Language Act of 2003* ([Northern Sami](/wiki/Northern_Sami_language "Northern Sami language"): *Sámi giellaláhka*; [Inari Sami](/wiki/Inari_Sami_language "Inari Sami language"): *Säämi kielâlaahâ*; [Skolt Sami](/wiki/Skolt_Sami_language "Skolt Sami language"): *Sääʹmǩiõll-lääʹǩǩ*; [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language"): *Saamen kielilaki*; [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Samisk språklag*) made Sami an official language in [Enontekiö](/wiki/Enonteki%C3%B6 "Enontekiö"), [Inari](/wiki/Inari,_Finland "Inari, Finland"), [Sodankylä](/wiki/Sodankyl%C3%A4 "Sodankylä") and [Utsjoki](/wiki/Utsjoki "Utsjoki") [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Finland "Municipalities of Finland"). Some documents, such as specific legislation, are translated into these Sami languages, but knowledge of any of these Sami languages among officials is not common. As the major language in the region is Finnish, Sami speakers are essentially always bilingual with Finnish. [Language nest](/wiki/Language_nest "Language nest") daycares have been set up for teaching the languages to children. In education, Northern Sami, and to a more limited degree, Inari and Skolt Sami, can be studied at primary and secondary levels, both as a mothertongue (for native speakers) and as a foreign language (for non-native speakers).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Samisktalande_i_Finland.jpg/250px-Samisktalande_i_Finland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samisktalande_i_Finland.jpg)

Sami speakers in Finland 1980-2010.

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Russia")]

In [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), Sámi has no official status, neither on the national, regional or local level. It is included in the list of Indigenous minority languages. (Kildin) Sami has been taught at the [Murmansk State Technical University](/wiki/Murmansk_State_Technical_University "Murmansk State Technical University") since 2012; before then, it was taught at the [Institute of the Peoples of the North](/wiki/Institute_of_the_Peoples_of_the_North "Institute of the Peoples of the North") in [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Lexicon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Lexicon")]

### Nordic countries and language names in Sámi languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A1mi_languages&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Nordic countries and language names in Sámi languages")]

The words below illustrate similarities and differences between the different Sámi languages. The cultural influence of the majority language spoken around the Sámi is also reflected in the words used for the different country names.

The word for language is almost identical across languages despite differences in spelling, /kielːa/, although in Skolt Sámi more changes have taken place /ˈciɤlː/. The words for "Finland" bear a resemblance not only to the word for Sápmi, but also to the Finnish word for their country, *Suomi*. On the other hand, the word for "Norwegian" /daru/ is etymologically related to the word meaning "foreigner"’. Interestingly, in both Ume and Lule Sámi, the same word is used for both Norwegian and Swedish, *dáruongiälla* and *dárogiella*, respectively.

|  | North | South | Lule | Ume | Inari | Skolt |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Sapmi** | *Sápmi* | *Saepmie* | *Sábme* | *Sábmie* | *Säämi* | *Sää′mjânnam* |
| **Finland, Finnish** | *Suopma, suomagiella* | *Soeme, soemengïele* | *Suobme, suomagiella* | NA | *Suomâ, suomâkielâ* | *Lää′d, lää′dǩiõll* |
| **Norway, Norwegian** | *Norga, dárogiella* | *Nöörje, daaroengïele* | *Vuodna, dárogiella* | *Nürjje, dáruongiälla* | *Taaža, tárukiellâ* | *Taarr, taarǩiõll* |
| **Russia, Russian** | *Ruošša, ruoššagiella* | *Russlaante* | NA | NA | *Ruoššâ, ruošâkielâ* | *Ruõššjânnam, ruõššǩiõll* |
| **Sweden, Swedish** | *Ruoŧŧa, ruoŧagiella* | *Sveerje, sveerjengïele* | *Svieria, dárogiella* | *Sverjje, dáruongiälla* | *Ruotâ, ruotâkielâ* | *Ruõcc, ruõccǩiõll* |
| **Sami (language)** | *sámegiella* | *saemiengïele* | *sámegiella* | *sámiengiëlla* | *sämekielâ* | *sää′mǩiõll* |
